text: The surgeon performs a careful digital examination to assess the severity of the anal stenosis. An SNM retractor is inserted, followed by an incision of the fibrotic tissue at the three o'clock position in the anal canal, providing a clear view of the area for flap positioning.
The surgeon gently detaches the mucosa of the lower rectum, exposing healthy internal tissue in preparation for a safe suture with a cutaneous flap.
The surgeon marks a rhomboid shape on the skin to indicate the size of the flap, followed by a precise cutaneous incision using a cold knife scalpel.

The surgeon incises the subcutaneous tissue using diathermy, ensuring a good blood supply by creating a flap with a wider base than the surface, resulting in a conical shape of the subcutaneous fatty tissue.
The surgeon carefully prepares the medial part of the flap, ensuring that the superficial layer of the external sphincter remains undamaged during the process.

The surgeon transposes the flap to the top of the anal canal and sutures it to the healthy mucosa using long-term absorbable stitches, ensuring secure attachment and integration with the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon closes the donor site, ensuring there is no dead space or tension on the internal suture. After closing the skin, the diameter and elasticity of the anal canal are evaluated to determine if a contralateral flap is necessary.
